findings.purpose: A 7-stage Python pipeline that ingests K-Beauty product metadata from OliveYoung and Beauty Kurly (via DynamoDB), groups similar products using BERT + DBSCAN clustering, extracts text from product images via Google Cloud Vision OCR, runs AI-powered feature analysis using OpenAI GPT models (o4-mini), and writes versioned PostgreSQL tables (product_raw, product_analysis, product_service) for downstream analytics.
findings.architecture: The pipeline is orchestrated entirely by run_steps.py via the PipelineRunner class. Steps 1-7 are discrete methods on that class, each accepting optional DataFrame inputs or falling back to checkpoint CSVs in results/. Lazy-loaded imports keep startup fast. Between every step the runner calls save_checkpoint() to persist a CSV in results/checkpoint_{step_name}.csv, so any step can be re-entered from disk without re-running prior steps. TableVersionManager (utils/tbl_version_manager.py — documented separately as the table-manager entry) controls all versioned PostgreSQL table creation and activation; run_steps.py calls version_manager.get_active_table() and version_manager.create_versioned_table() at Steps 2, 4, 6, and 7. External API calls are async throughout: DynamoDB parallel segment scans (20 segments), S3 image fetches, Vision API calls via ThreadPoolExecutor, and OpenAI Batch API submission via KeywordExtractor + BatchJobManager. The non-blocking Step 5 pattern (submit_batches → check_status → retrieve_results) lets operators submit GPU-heavy batch jobs to OpenAI and close their laptops, checking status later.

keyCommands.purpose: Load OliveYoung and BK metadata from DynamoDB into a combined, filtered checkpoint CSV.
Clean product names (GPT), group with BERT+DBSCAN+fuzzy matching, select representatives, write product_raw versioned table.
Run Google Cloud Vision OCR on representative product images from S3, storing results in DynamoDB ItemKeywords tables.
Fetch product images from S3 (base64) and OCR keywords from DynamoDB, producing analysis-ready checkpoint.
Submit AI analysis batches to OpenAI Batch API non-blockingly and return a session ID.
Poll batch job status from OpenAI for a given (or latest) session.
Download completed OpenAI batch results, run option matching, and save to checkpoint.
Run Step 5 in legacy blocking mode with a 10-item sample for local testing.
Match AI answers to option texts, combine with existing product_analysis data, and create a new versioned product_analysis table.
Compute item-level and group-level features (popularity_index, lifecycle_stage, vitality_score), write versioned product_service table.

notableDecisions.decision: Checkpoint CSV files between every step, allowing re-entry at any stage without re-running upstream
notableDecisions.rationale: Long-running API calls (OCR, GPT) make full reruns prohibitively expensive; checkpoints let operators restart after failures.

notableDecisions.decision: Non-blocking OpenAI Batch API workflow for Step 5 (submit → check → retrieve) instead of blocking async loop
notableDecisions.rationale: Analysis of ~28K products would take hours in a blocking loop; Batch API lets the operator submit and disconnect.
notableDecisions.evidence: run_steps.py:495-573 (step5_submit_batches), run_steps.py:564-571 (YOU CAN NOW CLOSE YOUR LAPTOP message)

notableDecisions.decision: Hybrid BERT-embeddings + DBSCAN + fuzzy-matching for product grouping instead of purely string-based dedup
notableDecisions.rationale: K-Beauty product names include size variants, shade codes (15.1A vs 15.1B), and promotional suffixes that fool pure string matching; the hybrid handles semantic and string similarity together.

notableDecisions.decision: OCR skip threshold: items with existing keywords >= 35 chars are not re-processed
notableDecisions.rationale: Incremental processing avoids redundant Vision API calls on already-extracted items.

notableDecisions.decision: TableVersionManager delegates all versioned-table lifecycle to a carved-out utility (documented as table-manager entry)
notableDecisions.rationale: Versioning logic is reusable across pipeline stages and warrants its own testable unit.
notableDecisions.evidence: run_steps.py:59-63 (version_manager property), src/database/tbl_version_manager.py (present but not re-documented here)

findings.dataFlow: DynamoDB (OliveYoung_MetaData + BK_MetaData) → Step 1 parallel segment scan → in-memory DataFrame → checkpoint_step1_metadata.csv → Step 2 BERT+DBSCAN grouping + representative selection → product_raw_{version} (PostgreSQL) + checkpoint_step2_grouped.csv → Step 3 Google Cloud Vision OCR on S3 images → DynamoDB (OliveYoung_ItemKeywords + BK_ItemKeywords) → Step 4 S3 image fetch + DynamoDB keyword fetch → checkpoint_step4_analysis_data.csv → Step 5 OpenAI Batch API (o4-mini) → results/final_results_{ts}.csv + checkpoint_step5_analysis_results.csv → Step 6 option matching + merge with existing product_analysis → product_analysis_{version} (PostgreSQL) → Step 7 feature engineering (popularity_index, lifecycle_stage, vitality_score) → product_service_{version} (PostgreSQL)

contentPointers.claim: Orchestrator entry point is run_steps.py; PipelineRunner class owns all 7 step methods

Step 1 performs parallel 20-segment DynamoDB scan of OliveYoung_MetaData and BK_MetaData, standardizes date formats to ISO 8601, maps BK brand IDs, filters by category_source table, deduplicates on itemId

Step 2 calls MetadataPreprocessor.process_updated_data() which chains GPT name cleaning (ProductNameCleaner), BERT embeddings (bert-base-multilingual-cased), DBSCAN clustering (eps=0.3, cosine metric), fuzzy matching (fuzzywuzzy, threshold 0.9), representative voting, then writes product_raw via DBConnector.update_product_raw_table()

Step 3 filters to is_representative==True rows, uses AsyncOCRExtractor with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=5) calling google.cloud.vision ImageAnnotatorClient, skips items with existing DynamoDB keywords >= 35 chars, stores results in OliveYoung_ItemKeywords or BK_ItemKeywords
Step 4 uses ImagePipeline.fetch_product_data() to fetch S3 images (base64) and OCR keywords from DynamoDB, maps category_id_main, skips group_ids already in active product_analysis table

Step 5 non-blocking path: KeywordExtractor.submit_batches() writes batch jobs to OpenAI Batch API and returns a session_id; status checked later via check_status(); results downloaded via retrieve_results() which also calls process_results_with_matching()
KeywordExtractor loads category-specific question/option data from PostgreSQL (questions + options tables) and builds prompts using evaluation_criteria.json and common_prompt_v5.txt

Step 6 instantiates ProductAnalysisProcessor with active product_analysis table name, calls process_data_with_separate_rows() on new results, concatenates with existing data, creates a new versioned product_analysis table via TableVersionManager and activates it

Step 7 calls generate_features() (item-level: size_rounded, size_category, price_category, discount_percentage, price_per_unit, review_density, popularity_index) then transform_to_product_service() (group-level: lifecycle_stage, vitality_score, weighted_rating), writes versioned product_service table
S3 bucket name 'trendmix' is hardcoded in db_connector.py (two locations)
CLI supports --from-db flag on Steps 3 and 4 to load from active product_raw PostgreSQL table instead of checkpoint CSV
variables/.env.sample exists and documents the 6 required env vars

suggestedSections: Pipeline overview — purpose and what downstream systems consume the outputs
The 7 stages — one subsection per stage with input, mechanism, output, and evidence
Entry point and CLI — run_steps.py usage, all flags including Step 5 async workflow
External dependencies — Google Cloud Vision, OpenAI Batch API, DynamoDB tables, S3 bucket
Versioned PostgreSQL tables — product_raw / product_analysis / product_service and their relation to TableVersionManager
Checkpoint system — how CSV checkpoints enable per-step re-entry

gapsFromBrief: Brief asks to verify CLI commands (verified: true) — pipeline requires live DynamoDB + PostgreSQL + S3 + Google Vision + OpenAI credentials not available in this research context; all commands marked verified: false

Brief mentions `--target` and version args as CLI entry points — run_steps.py uses --step and --from-db but no --target or --version flag was found; PIPELINE_FLOWS.md and README do not mention them either. This may refer to TableVersionManager's own CLI (documented in src/database/TableVersionManagerCLI.md and covered by the table-manager agent-docs entry), not run_steps.py

The brief's emphasis list uses the term 'stage 1 data loading' through 'stage 7 feature engineering' — the actual run_steps.py and PIPELINE_FLOWS.md number the stages consistently as steps 1-7 with that same mapping, so no gap. However PIPELINE_FLOWS.md enumerates 9 stages (it splits grouping/name-cleaning and adds a Database Storage stage 9) while run_steps.py consolidates to 7; the 7-stage framing in the brief matches run_steps.py exactly

batch_job_manager.py (src/feature_extraction/batch_job_manager.py) was not read in detail — it is marked private (chmod 600); its API surface is accessed via KeywordExtractor.batch_manager and is secondary to the brief's focus
recommendations: The two GCP service account JSON files (variables/trendmix-f352cfcaf05f.json, variables/vision-test-app-363706-e9e75b6057bf.json) are present on disk but gitignored via the variables/ rule — safe from accidental commit, but the project ID 'vision-test-app-363706' is visible in PIPELINE_FLOWS.md:236 (public doc). This is a low-severity info-leak to flag in knownIssues.

S3 bucket name 'trendmix' is hardcoded in src/database/db_connector.py:119 and :312 rather than read from the S3_BUCKET_NAME env var that is already defined. This is inconsistent and should be a knownIssue.

run_pipeline.py (the original Prefect-orchestrated scraping orchestrator) is gitignored — it exists only locally. The agent-docs entry should note that scraping is handled by separate upstream projects (oliveyoung-scraper, bkurly-scraper) and that this repo covers only the post-scraping 7 stages.

The requirements.txt has two editable local installs (-e /Users/llnormll/PythonPackages/bkurly-scraper and -e /Users/llnormll/PythonPackages/oliveyoung-scraper) with absolute paths — these will silently fail for any other machine. Flag as a setup issue in knownIssues.
